graphic.name: three-towns
graphic.caption: Three towns need one signal tower that reaches all of them equally. Where do you build it?

steps.body:
Three towns want a single tower that is the same distance from all three — equidistant just means "equally far from each."
That perfect spot is the circumcenter, O. A circle centred at O passes right through all three towns at once.
The circle through all three corners of a triangle is its circumscribed circle, and O is its centre.

steps.prompt: Drag the corners and watch the three distances
steps.subtitle: Compare [OA, OB, and OC](green) in the readout as the triangle changes shape.

steps.prompt: Wherever you drag, the centre O is…

options.label: the same distance from all three corners.
the average of the three corners (the centroid).
options.feedback: The centroid (the average) usually is NOT equally far from the corners — watch OA, OB, OC stay equal here instead.

options.label: closest to the largest angle.
options.feedback: Watch the readouts: OA, OB, and OC stay equal no matter the shape.

feedback.correct: Exactly. O stays the same distance from all three corners, so one circle catches all three.
feedback.incorrect: Compare the three distance readouts — they stay equal as you drag.

steps.body:
A perpendicular bisector of a side is the line that cuts it in half at a right angle. Every point on it is equally far from that side's two endpoints.
The three [perpendicular bisectors](orange) of a triangle's sides all pass through one point — the circumcenter O.
Since O sits on every bisector, it is the same distance from all three corners, so one circle centred at O passes through them all.

steps.prompt: Why the bisectors meet at one point

gates.prompt: Which line is exactly the set of points equally far from A and B?
gates.hint: "Equally far from two points" is the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining them.

gates.prompt: We draw a second such line. For which side?

choices.label: BC — its perpendicular bisector (points equally far from B and C).

gates.hint: Use a second side to pin the point down.

gates.prompt: The two bisectors cross at O. What does lying on both tell us?

choices.label: OA = OB and OB = OC, so OA = OB = OC.
O is the midpoint of AB.

gates.hint: Each bisector keeps O equally far from that side's two endpoints.

gates.prompt: Since OA = OB = OC, the circle centred at O…

choices.label: is tangent to all three sides.

gates.hint: All three corners are the same distance from O — exactly the radius.

stages: Start with triangle ABC. We want a single circle through all three corners.
The perpendicular bisector of AB is exactly the points equally far from A and B. Draw it.
Likewise, the perpendicular bisector of BC is exactly the points equally far from B and C.
They cross at O. Lying on both, O has OA = OB and OB = OC, so OA = OB = OC.
So a circle centred at O reaches all three corners — and the third bisector, of CA, runs through O too.

steps.prompt: What's true at the circumcenter O?
steps.subtitle: Select every statement that is always true of the circumcenter.

options.label: [OA = OB = OC](green) — the same distance to all three corners.
A circle centred at O passes through all three corners.
O lies on the perpendicular bisector of every side.
O is always inside the triangle.
O is the average position of the three corners (the centroid).

feedback.correct: Yes. O is equidistant from the corners, sits on all three bisectors, and is the centre of the circle through them.
feedback.incorrect: Two are off: O can fall OUTSIDE an obtuse triangle, and it is NOT the centroid (the average of the corners).

steps.body:
If the triangle has a right angle, its hypotenuse (the longest side, opposite the right angle) is a diameter of the circumscribed circle. (That's Thales' theorem, run backwards.)
So the radius is simply half the hypotenuse — no bisectors needed.
A 6–8–10 right triangle has hypotenuse 10, so its circumscribed circle has [radius](green) 5.

steps.prompt: Find the slip in this working
steps.subtitle: A student locates the circumcenter O of triangle ABC. One line is wrong — tap it.

lines.text: O must be the same distance from A, B, and C.
The point equally far from all three is the average of A, B, and C.
So O is the centroid, the average of the corners.
O is found by averaging the three corners' positions.

steps.explanation: Equidistant does NOT mean the average. The average of the corners is the centroid, which usually is NOT the same distance from all three. The circumcenter is where the perpendicular bisectors meet.
steps.hint: Is the average of the corners really equally far from each corner? Try a long, thin triangle.
steps.doneNote: Right — the average is the centroid, not the circumcenter.

problems.prompt: A right triangle has a hypotenuse of 10. Find the radius of its circumscribed circle.

feedback.correct: Correct. The hypotenuse is a diameter, so the radius is 10 ÷ 2 = 5.
feedback.incorrect: In a right triangle the hypotenuse is a diameter, so the circumradius is half of it.

problems.prompt: A right triangle has legs 10 and 24. Find the radius of its circumscribed circle.

feedback.correct: Correct. The hypotenuse is √(10² + 24²) = 26, so the radius is 13.
feedback.incorrect: First find the hypotenuse with the Pythagorean theorem, then halve it.

problems.prompt: The circumscribed circle of a right triangle has radius 9. Find the hypotenuse.

feedback.correct: Correct. The hypotenuse is the diameter, twice the radius: 2 × 9 = 18.
feedback.incorrect: The hypotenuse is a diameter, so it is twice the radius.

steps.subtitle: A right triangle has a hypotenuse of 14. Find the [radius](green) of its circumscribed circle.

feedback.correct: Done. The hypotenuse is a diameter, so the radius is 14 ÷ 2 = 7.
feedback.incorrect: The hypotenuse is a diameter, so the circumradius is half of it.
